content.full_text:
May 10, 1990

Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA95804

Dear Mr. Wallace:

I am writing per our conversation to obtain a formal FPPC ruling on whether or not I have a conflict of interest on an upcoming San Carlos City Council issue. As you will recall, I serve on the San Carlos Council and am employed full time by PG&E.

The Council will consider on May 29 a motion to place on the November 1990 ballot a utility users tax. This tax is noted seperately on utility bills. It is collected by PG&E (and other impacted utilities) and then disbursed to the cities. PG&E incurs no administrative costs if cities use the attached model ordinance enacting a users tax with no exemptions or special conditions. If low income seniors are exempted and fees are capped at $10,000 (such as has been proposed in San Carlos), then PG&E does incur some programming costs. While the exact cost is not available, it is certainly less than the $250,000 expense mentioned in the attached regulation 18702.2(2).

My impression from reading this regulation and from talking with the City Attorney and a PG&E attorney is that I do not have a conflict of interest since the tax would not have a direct, material or significant impact on PG&E. I would like this confirmed or denied by the FPPC so that I can act or abstain with some degree of certainty.

By copy of this letter, I am asking the City Attorney and PG&E attorney to forward their opinions in writing to me by May 16. I will mail these opinions to you to assist in making a ruling.

Since the Council has scheduled a vote on this issue on May 29, I would appreciate a written opinion no later than May 25. If this is not sufficient time, please communicate a formal opinion verbally.

Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

Paul Sivley
39 Vine Street
San Carlos, CA 94070
(415) 592 3420 (h)
(415) 972 6534 (w)


cc:    Mike Garvey/City Manager
       Joe Kelly/PG&E Attorney
       Kevin Kelly/San Carlos Mayor
       Bob Lanzone/City Attorney

90-343
JW
FPPC
MAY 21 1932 AM '90

May 18, 1990


Mr. John Wallace
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA95804

Dear Mr. Wallace:

Per my letter of May 10, I am forwarding a written opinion I received from
the San Carlos City Attorney regarding my potential conflict of interest on a
utility users tax vote before the San Carlos City Council.

I still am lacking three pieces of information:

*        The FPPC ruling on whether I have a conflict of interest.
*        Whether the cost trigger for direct material impact to PG&E is
         $250,000 or $100,000.
*        The administrative cost to PG&E of implementing this tax.

I am trying to find the answer to the last two questions from PG&E personnel.
Any information I receive will be sent on to you. I appreciate your help on
the first item, and I hope the attached will be of assistance.


Sincerely,

Paul Sivley
39 Vine Street
San Carlos, CA  94070
(415) 592  3420 (h)
(415) 972  6534 (w)

LAW OFFICES

AARONSON, DICKERSON, COHN & LANZONE
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

939 LAUREL STREET, SUITE D
POST OFFICE BOX 1065
SAN CARLOS, CALIFORNIA 94070
415-593-3117

KENNETH M. DICKERSON
ROBERT J. LANZONE
MARC L ZAFFERANO
JEAN S. SAVAREE
GREGORY J. RUBENS

OF COUNSEL
MICHAEL AARONSON
MELVIN E. COHN

May 16, 1990


TO:     PAUL SIVLEY, COUNCILMEMBER, CITY OF SAN CARLOS

FROM:   ROBERT J. LANZONE, CITY ATTORNEY

RE:     POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

Dear Paul:

     In your letter of May 10, 1990, to the FPPC you asked,
indirectly, for my opinion as City Attorney on your potential
conflict of interest in voting on the issue of a Utility User Tax.
My understanding is that you are an employee of Pacific Gas &
Electric, which is a utility company doing business in the City of
San Carlos.

     Under Government Code §87103, subparagraph (d), there is
no question that you have a prima facie conflict of interest in
that you are an employee of a utility company that is indirectly
affected by the decision on the utility user tax. The question is
whether the decision will have a material financial effect
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. You did
include with your material a copy of FPPC Regulation §18702.2 which
deals with business entities indirectly involved or affected by a
decision. Under that Code Section a business entity such as PG&E
that incurs expenses through utility user tax in the amount of
$100,000. or more would certainly be materially affected. I am not
sure if that is the correct subparagraph that applies to PG&E on
the sliding scale set forth in the regulations.

     There remains an issue as to whether or not such a
decision is held to have an effect your position and chance of
promotion within PG&E. If there would be such an effect it might
be held to be material.

     Finally, it seems to me that this must have been decided
for an elected official, who was an employee of a utility company
in one of the other jurisdictions in California that have adopted

Mr. Paul Sivley
May 16, 1990
Page 2


a utility user tax. This cannot be the first time it has occurred.
I realize the urgency of your having to find out the answer to this
question. We will follow through with the FPPC to see if, in fact,
they have written an opinion letter on this very issue before. I
would think the chances of their having done that are very good.

If you have any questions please let me know.

Sincerely,

ROBERT J. LANZONE

RJL:bg

cc: Michael P. Garvey
